graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to give the *complete* procedural requirements for real-world testing of a high-risk AI system **outside** a regulatory sandbox (e.g., the full step-by-step process, who authorises it, and all documentation/notifications), because the excerpts focus on real‑world testing mainly **in connection with** regulatory sandboxes and only partially describe the “outside sandbox” case.

From the context I *can* confirm the following interaction and requirements/safeguards:

- Interaction with sandbox provisions:
  - “Testing those systems in real world conditions” **does not require participation** in an AI regulatory sandbox.
  - However, **upon agreement** between national competent authorities and sandbox participants, “testing in real world conditions may also be operated and supervised in the framework of the AI regulatory sandbox.”
  - In any case where real‑world testing is supervised within a sandbox, it occurs under **supervision by national competent authorities** and the sandbox operates **in accordance with** GDPR (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) and Reg. (EU) 2018/1725, and **without prejudice** to Directive (EU) 2016/680.

- Procedural requirements the context explicitly mentions for prospective providers doing real‑world testing (whether or not in a sandbox):
  - Have a **real‑world testing plan** that is **submitted to the competent market surveillance authority**.
  - **Register** the testing “in dedicated sections in the EU database,” subject to “some limited exceptions.”
  - Testing must be **time‑limited** (“set limitations on the period for which the testing can be done”).
  - There should be **effective oversight by competent personnel** involved in the real‑world testing.
  - If **significant risks** are identified during development/testing, this must lead to **adequate mitigation**; failing that, **suspension** of the development/testing process.

- Specific safeguards: informed consent, data protection, vulnerable groups, reversibility
  - Informed consent:
    - The context references a requirement of **“requesting informed consent” for testing in real world conditions**, and also notes this is **“without prejudice to consent of data subjects”** under applicable data protection law. (So consent requirements follow/align with the relevant data protection regime, not replace it.)
  - Data protection:
    - Personal data must be **protected** under Union data protection law; principles such as **data minimisation** and **data protection by design and by default** apply when personal data is processed.
    - Personal data must be **deleted when subjects have withdrawn** (withdrawal of consent triggers deletion in the excerpts).
    - The Regulation provides a legal basis, in the sandbox context, for providers/prospective providers to use personal data collected for other purposes to develop certain AI systems in the public interest *within* a sandbox, but the excerpt is incomplete on the exact conditions—so I can’t state the full rule beyond that.
  - Vulnerable groups:
    - Authorities may require “**additional safeguards** for persons belonging to certain vulnerable groups.”
    - Additional safeguards are described as protecting vulnerable persons and protecting personal data; they also ensure that the AI system’s **predictions, recommendations, or decisions can be effectively reversed and disregarded**.

If you want me to produce a “complete procedural checklist” for the **outside-sandbox** scenario, please share the relevant article/recital text covering that section (or more pages from the document), because the provided context only gives partial procedural elements and does not include the full external authorisation/notification conditions.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: A prospective provider wants to test a high-risk AI system in real-world conditions outside a regulatory sandbox. What are the complete procedural requirements, how do these interact with sandbox provisions, and what specific safeguards apply regarding informed consent, data protection, and vulnerable groups?

From the context I *can* confirm the following interaction and requirements/safeguards:

- Interaction with sandbox provisions:
  - “Testing those systems in real world conditions” **does not require participation** in an AI regulatory sandbox.
  - However, **upon agreement** between national competent authorities and sandbox participants, “testing in real world conditions may also be operated and supervised in the framework of the AI regulatory sandbox.”
  - In any case where real‑world testing is supervised within a sandbox, it occurs under **supervision by national competent authorities** and the sandbox operates **in accordance with** GDPR (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) and Reg. (EU) 2018/1725, and **without prejudice** to Directive (EU) 2016/680.

  - Have a **real‑world testing plan** that is **submitted to the competent market surveillance authority**.
  - **Register** the testing “in dedicated sections in the EU database,” subject to “some limited exceptions.”

  - Informed consent:

(So consent requirements follow/align with the relevant data protection regime, not replace it.)
  - Data protection:
    - Personal data must be **protected** under Union data protection law; principles such as **data minimisation** and **data protection by design and by default** apply when personal data is processed.
    - Personal data must be **deleted when subjects have withdrawn** (withdrawal of consent triggers deletion in the excerpts).
    - The Regulation provides a legal basis, in the sandbox context, for providers/prospective providers to use personal data collected for other purposes to develop certain AI systems in the public interest *within* a sandbox, but the excerpt is incomplete on the exact conditions—so I can’t state the full rule beyond that.
  - Vulnerable groups:
    - Authorities may require “**additional safeguards** for persons belonging to certain vulnerable groups.”
    - Additional safeguards are described as protecting vulnerable persons and protecting personal data; they also ensure that the AI system’s **predictions, recommendations, or decisions can be effectively reversed and disregarded**.

If you want me to produce a “complete procedural checklist” for the **outside-sandbox** scenario, please share the relevant article/recital text covering that section (or more pages from the document), because the provided context only gives partial procedural elements and does not include the full external authorisation/notification conditions.

Document start
This Document belongs to the source EUAIAct-1-44.pdf
Content: Text Content:
 for the purpose of ensuring compliance of AI systems with this  Regulation. Upon agreement between the national competent authorities and the participants in the AI regulatory  sandbox, testing in real world conditions may also be operated and supervised in the framework of the AI regulatory  sandbox. (140) This Regulation should provide the legal basis for the providers and prospective providers in the AI regulatory  sandbox to use personal data collected for other purposes for developing certain AI systems in the public interest  within the AI regulatory sandbox, only under
----
 occurrence of substantial modification that may require a new  conformity assessment procedure. Any significant risks identified during the development and testing of such AI  systems should result in adequate mitigation and, failing that, in the suspension of the development and testing  process. Where appropriate, national competent authorities establishing AI regulatory sandboxes should cooperate  with other relevant authorities, including those supervising the protection of fundamental rights, and could allow for  the involvement of other actors within the AI ecosystem such as national or European standardisation organisations
----
 the period for which the testing can be done and  require additional safeguards for persons belonging to certain vulnerable groups, as well as a written agreement  defining the roles and responsibilities of prospective providers and deployers and effective oversight by competent  personnel involved in the real world testing. Furthermore, it is appropriate to envisage additional safeguards to  ensure that the predictions, recommendations or decisions of the AI system can be effectively reversed and  disregarded and that personal data is protected and is deleted when the subjects have withdrawn
----
 without prejudice to, consent of data subjects for the  processing of their personal data under the relevant data protection law. It is also important to minimise the risks  and enable oversight by competent authorities and therefore require prospective providers to have a real-world  testing plan submitted to competent market surveillance authority, register the testing in dedicated sections in the EU  database subject to some limited exceptions, set limitations on the period for which the testing can be done and  require additional safeguards for persons belonging to certain vulnerable
----

chunks.text: for the purpose of ensuring compliance of AI systems with this Regulation. Upon agreement between the national competent authorities and the participants in the AI regulatory sandbox, testing in real world conditions may also be operated and supervised in the framework of the AI regulatory sandbox. (140) This Regulation should provide the legal basis for the providers and prospective providers in the AI regulatory sandbox to use personal data collected for other purposes for developing certain AI systems in the public interest within the AI regulatory sandbox, only under

occurrence of substantial modification that may require a new conformity assessment procedure. Any significant risks identified during the development and testing of such AI systems should result in adequate mitigation and, failing that, in the suspension of the development and testing process. Where appropriate, national competent authorities establishing AI regulatory sandboxes should cooperate with other relevant authorities, including those supervising the protection of fundamental rights, and could allow for the involvement of other actors within the AI ecosystem such as national or European standardisation organisations

the period for which the testing can be done and require additional safeguards for persons belonging to certain vulnerable groups, as well as a written agreement defining the roles and responsibilities of prospective providers and deployers and effective oversight by competent personnel involved in the real world testing. Furthermore, it is appropriate to envisage additional safeguards to ensure that the predictions, recommendations or decisions of the AI system can be effectively reversed and disregarded and that personal data is protected and is deleted when the subjects have withdrawn

without prejudice to, consent of data subjects for the processing of their personal data under the relevant data protection law. It is also important to minimise the risks and enable oversight by competent authorities and therefore require prospective providers to have a real-world testing plan submitted to competent market surveillance authority, register the testing in dedicated sections in the EU database subject to some limited exceptions, set limitations on the period for which the testing can be done and require additional safeguards for persons belonging to certain vulnerable
